name: Irish Stout
description: Dry, roasty, sessionable. The Guinness archetype — black as night, easy as a Tuesday afternoon. Roasted barley does most of the work. Ideal first dark beer because the strong roast character covers minor brewing flaws.

packaging._note: Traditionally served low-carb on nitro. If kegging, nitro and stout faucet are the dream.

expected_profile.overall_summary: Coffee, dark chocolate, dry-roast finish. The oats add a silky body that softens the roasted barley's sharpness. Surprisingly light and drinkable for something this dark.

tips: Roasted barley (not roasted MALT) is the key. They taste different — sharper, drier.
The flaked oats make a real difference to mouthfeel. Don't skip them.
Stout is forgiving. The strong roast character masks minor faults — perfect for early brews.
If you can serve on nitro, do it. Otherwise low carbonation gets close to the cask experience.
Improves with 2-3 weeks of conditioning — flavours integrate and the roast bitterness softens.
common_mistakes: Using chocolate malt instead of roasted barley (different beer entirely — closer to a porter)
Over-carbonating (stout should be soft, not sharp)
Serving fridge-cold (suppresses the roast character — let it warm up to ~8-10°C)
Adding too much roasted barley (300g is plenty — more becomes acrid)
